title: Galactic pole

infobox.value: (**GCS**) is a [celestial coordinate system](/wiki/Celestial_coordinate_system "Celestial coordinate system") in [spherical coordinates](/wiki/Spherical_coordinates "Spherical coordinates"), with the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") as its center, the primary direction aligned with the approximate center of the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") [Galaxy](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy"), and the [fundamental plane](/wiki/Fundamental_plane_(spherical_coordinates) "Fundamental plane (spherical coordinates)") parallel to an approximation of the [galactic plane](/wiki/Galactic_plane "Galactic plane") but offset to its north. It uses the [right-handed convention](/wiki/Right-hand_rule "Right-hand rule"), meaning that coordinates are positive toward the north and toward the east in the [fundamental plane](/wiki/Fundamental_plane_(spherical_coordinates) "Fundamental plane (spherical coordinates)").[[1]](#cite_note-Blaauwetal-1)
+90° latitude | 12h 51.4m | +27.13° | [Coma Berenices](/wiki/Coma_Berenices "Coma Berenices") (near [31 Com](/wiki/31_Comae_Berenices "31 Comae Berenices")) |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Galactic pole](/w/index.php?title=Galactic_pole&redirect=no "Galactic pole"))

Celestial coordinate system in spherical coordinates, with the Sun as its center

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Artist%27s_impression_of_the_Milky_Way_%28updated_-_annotated%29.jpg/330px-Artist%27s_impression_of_the_Milky_Way_%28updated_-_annotated%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Artist%27s_impression_of_the_Milky_Way_(updated_-_annotated).jpg)

An artistic depiction of the Milky Way Galaxy showing the origin and orientation of galactic longitude. The galactic longitude (*l*) runs from the Sun upwards in the image through the center of the galaxy. The galactic latitude (*b*) is perpendicular to the image (i.e. coming out of the image) and also centered on the Sun.

The **galactic coordinate system** (**GCS**) is a [celestial coordinate system](/wiki/Celestial_coordinate_system "Celestial coordinate system") in [spherical coordinates](/wiki/Spherical_coordinates "Spherical coordinates"), with the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") as its center, the primary direction aligned with the approximate center of the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") [Galaxy](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy"), and the [fundamental plane](/wiki/Fundamental_plane_(spherical_coordinates) "Fundamental plane (spherical coordinates)") parallel to an approximation of the [galactic plane](/wiki/Galactic_plane "Galactic plane") but offset to its north. It uses the [right-handed convention](/wiki/Right-hand_rule "Right-hand rule"), meaning that coordinates are positive toward the north and toward the east in the [fundamental plane](/wiki/Fundamental_plane_(spherical_coordinates) "Fundamental plane (spherical coordinates)").[[1]](#cite_note-Blaauwetal-1)

## Spherical coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Galactic_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Spherical coordinates")]

### Galactic longitude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Galactic_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Galactic longitude")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Galactic_coordinates.JPG/250px-Galactic_coordinates.JPG)](/wiki/File:Galactic_coordinates.JPG)

The galactic coordinates use the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") as the origin. Galactic longitude (*l*) is measured with primary direction from the Sun to the center of the galaxy in the galactic plane, while the galactic latitude (*b*) measures the [angle](/wiki/Angle "Angle") of the object above the [galactic plane](/wiki/Galactic_plane "Galactic plane").

Longitude (symbol *l*) measures the [angular distance](/wiki/Angle "Angle") of an object eastward along the galactic equator from the Galactic Center. Analogous to terrestrial [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), galactic longitude is usually measured in degrees (°).

### Galactic latitude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Galactic_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Galactic latitude")]

Latitude (symbol *b*) measures the [angle](/wiki/Angle "Angle") of an object northward of the galactic equator (or midplane) as viewed from Earth. Analogous to terrestrial [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), galactic latitude is usually measured in degrees.

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Galactic_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Definition")]

See also: [Galactic plane](/wiki/Galactic_plane "Galactic plane"), [Galactic Center](/wiki/Galactic_Center "Galactic Center"), and [Galactic anticenter](/wiki/Galactic_anticenter "Galactic anticenter")

The first galactic coordinate system was used by [William Herschel](/wiki/William_Herschel "William Herschel") in 1785. A number of different coordinate systems, each differing by a few degrees, were used until 1932, when [Lund Observatory](/wiki/Lund_Observatory "Lund Observatory") assembled a set of conversion tables that defined a standard galactic coordinate system based on a galactic north pole at [RA](/wiki/Right_ascension "Right ascension") 12h 40m, [dec](/wiki/Declination "Declination") +28° (in the [B1900.0](/wiki/B1900.0 "B1900.0") epoch convention) and a 0° longitude at the point where the [galactic plane](/wiki/Galactic_plane "Galactic plane") and [equatorial plane](/wiki/Equatorial_plane "Equatorial plane") intersected.[[1]](#cite_note-Blaauwetal-1)

In 1958, the [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union") (IAU) defined the galactic coordinate system in reference to radio observations of galactic neutral [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") through the [hydrogen line](/wiki/Hydrogen_line "Hydrogen line"), changing the definition of the Galactic longitude by 32° and the latitude by 1.5°.[[1]](#cite_note-Blaauwetal-1) In the [equatorial coordinate system](/wiki/Equatorial_coordinate_system "Equatorial coordinate system"), for [equinox and equator of 1950.0](/wiki/Epoch_(astronomy) "Epoch (astronomy)"), the north galactic pole is defined at [right ascension](/wiki/Right_ascension "Right ascension") 12h 49m, [declination](/wiki/Declination "Declination") +27.4°, in the constellation [Coma Berenices](/wiki/Coma_Berenices "Coma Berenices"), with a probable error of ±0.1°.[[2]](#cite_note-Reidetal-2) Longitude 0° is the great semicircle that originates from this point along the line in [position angle](/wiki/Position_angle "Position angle") 123° with respect to the [equatorial pole](/wiki/Celestial_pole "Celestial pole"). The galactic longitude increases in the same direction as right ascension. Galactic latitude is positive towards the north galactic pole, with a plane passing through the Sun and parallel to the galactic equator being 0°, whilst the poles are ±90°.[[3]](#cite_note-Binney-3) Based on this definition, the galactic poles and equator can be found from [spherical trigonometry](/wiki/Spherical_trigonometry "Spherical trigonometry") and can be [precessed](/wiki/Axial_precession "Axial precession") to other [epochs](/wiki/Epoch_(astronomy) "Epoch (astronomy)"); see the table.

**[J2000.0](/wiki/Epoch_(astronomy) "Epoch (astronomy)") [equatorial coordinates](/wiki/Equatorial_coordinate_system "Equatorial coordinate system") approximating the galactic reference points**[[1]](#cite_note-Blaauwetal-1)

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | **[Right ascension](/wiki/Right_ascension "Right ascension")** | **[Declination](/wiki/Declination "Declination")** | **[Constellation](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation")** |
| **North Pole** +90° latitude | 12h 51.4m | +27.13° | [Coma Berenices](/wiki/Coma_Berenices "Coma Berenices") (near [31 Com](/wiki/31_Comae_Berenices "31 Comae Berenices")) |
| **South Pole** −90° latitude | 0h 51.4m | −27.13° | [Sculptor](/wiki/Sculptor_(constellation) "Sculptor (constellation)") (near [NGC 288](/wiki/NGC_288 "NGC 288")) |
| **[Center](/wiki/Galactic_Center "Galactic Center")** 0° longitude | 17h 45.6m | −28.94° | [Sagittarius](/wiki/Sagittarius_(constellation) "Sagittarius (constellation)") (in [Sagittarius A](/wiki/Sagittarius_A "Sagittarius A")) |
| **[Anticenter](/wiki/Galactic_anticenter "Galactic anticenter")** 180° longitude | 5h 45.6m | +28.94° | [Auriga](/wiki/Auriga_(constellation) "Auriga (constellation)") (near [HIP](/wiki/Hipparcos "Hipparcos") 27180) |
| | Galactic north | Galactic south | Galactic center | | --- | --- | --- | | | | |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/2MASS_LSS_chart-NEW_Nasa-added_quadrants.jpg/250px-2MASS_LSS_chart-NEW_Nasa-added_quadrants.jpg)](/wiki/File:2MASS_LSS_chart-NEW_Nasa-added_quadrants.jpg)

Approx galactic quadrants (NGQ/SGQ, 1–4) indicated, along with differentiating Galactic Plane (containing the Galactic Center) and the Galactic Coordinates Plane (containing the Sun / Solar System)

The IAU recommended that during the transition period from the old, pre-1958 system to the new, the old longitude and latitude should be designated *l*I and *b*I while the new should be designated *l*II and *b*II.[[3]](#cite_note-Binney-3) This convention is occasionally seen.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[Radio source](/wiki/Radio_astronomy "Radio astronomy") [Sagittarius A\*](/wiki/Sagittarius_A* "Sagittarius A*"), which is the best physical marker of the true [Galactic Center](/wiki/Galactic_Center "Galactic Center"), is located at 17h 45m 40.0409s, −29° 00′ 28.118″ (J2000).[[2]](#cite_note-Reidetal-2) Rounded to the same number of digits as the table, 17h 45.7m, −29.01° (J2000), there is an offset of about 0.07° from the defined coordinate center, well within the 1958 error estimate of ±0.1°. Due to the Sun's position, which currently lies 56.75±6.20 [ly](/wiki/Light-year "Light-year") north of the midplane, and the heliocentric definition adopted by the IAU, the galactic coordinates of Sgr A\* are latitude -0° 07′ 12″, longitude +0° 04′ 06″. Since as defined the galactic coordinate system does not rotate with time, Sgr A\* is actually decreasing in longitude at the rate of galactic rotation at the sun, Ω, approximately 5.7 [milliarcseconds](/wiki/Milliarcsecond "Milliarcsecond") per year (see [Oort constants](/wiki/Oort_constants "Oort constants")).

## Conversion between equatorial and galactic coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Galactic_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Conversion between equatorial and galactic coordinates")]

An object's location expressed in the [equatorial coordinate system](/wiki/Equatorial_coordinate_system "Equatorial coordinate system") can be transformed into the galactic coordinate system. In these equations, *α* is [right ascension](/wiki/Right_ascension "Right ascension"), *δ* is [declination](/wiki/Declination "Declination"). NGP refers to the coordinate values of the north galactic pole and NCP to those of the north celestial pole.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\sin(b)&=\sin(\delta _{\text{NGP}})\sin(\delta )+\cos(\delta _{\text{NGP}})\cos(\delta )\cos(\alpha -\alpha _{\text{NGP}})\\\cos(b)\sin(l_{\text{NCP}}-l)&=\cos(\delta )\sin(\alpha -\alpha _{\text{NGP}})\\\cos(b)\cos(l_{\text{NCP}}-l)&=\cos(\delta _{\text{NGP}})\sin(\delta )-\sin(\delta _{\text{NGP}})\cos(\delta )\cos(\alpha -\alpha _{\text{NGP}})\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f5d268278fcf572144681132fdda9017f55f61f6)

The reverse (galactic to equatorial) can also be accomplished with the following conversion formulas.
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\sin(\delta )&=\sin(\delta _{\text{NGP}})\sin(b)+\cos(\delta _{\text{NGP}})\cos(b)\cos(l_{\text{NCP}}-l)\\\cos(\delta )\sin(\alpha -\alpha _{\text{NGP}})&=\cos(b)\sin(l_{\text{NCP}}-l)\\\cos(\delta )\cos(\alpha -\alpha _{\text{NGP}})&=\cos(\delta _{\text{NGP}})\sin(b)-\sin(\delta _{\text{NGP}})\cos(b)\cos(l_{\text{NCP}}-l)\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c9c8e90cae472eb2c88e98950984dd262b48c008)

Where:

![{\displaystyle \alpha _{\text{NGP}}=12^{h}51.4^{m}\qquad \delta _{\text{NGP}}=27.13^{\circ }\qquad l_{\text{NCP}}=122.93314^{\circ }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3b903a6e7381e99e3bd53f22a1c37d81727af436)

## Rectangular coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Galactic_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rectangular coordinates")]

In some applications use is made of rectangular coordinates based on galactic longitude and latitude and distance. In some work regarding the distant past or future the galactic coordinate system is taken as rotating so that the *x*-axis always goes to the centre of the galaxy.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

There are two major [rectangular](/wiki/Cartesian_coordinate_system "Cartesian coordinate system") variations of galactic coordinates, commonly used for computing space velocities of galactic objects. In these systems the *xyz*-axes are designated *UVW*, but the definitions vary by author. In one system, the *U* axis is directed toward the Galactic Center (*l* = 0°), and it is a [right-handed](/wiki/Right-hand_rule "Right-hand rule") system (positive towards the east and towards the north galactic pole); in the other, the *U* axis is directed toward the galactic anticenter (*l* = 180°), and it is a left-handed system (positive towards the east and towards the north galactic pole).[[7]](#cite_note-Johnson-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Milky_Way_infrared.jpg/250px-Milky_Way_infrared.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milky_Way_infrared.jpg)

The [anisotropy](/wiki/Anisotropy "Anisotropy") of the star density in the night sky makes the galactic coordinate system very useful for coordinating surveys, both those that require high densities of stars at low galactic latitudes, and those that require a low density of stars at high galactic latitudes. For this image the [Mollweide projection](/wiki/Mollweide_projection "Mollweide projection") has been applied, typical in maps using galactic coordinates.
